what_it_does: This file is a FINDS register that inventories an already-built graph-of-minds substrate in a fork segment, listing addressable node types, graph edges, session-lens projections, control verbs, onboarding and channel wiring, durable records, and the exposed gap that no rendered Face consumes the substrate yet.
observations: The substrate defines a unified address grammar for session, clone, mind, run/composition-step, and board nodes.
Each listed capability carries an explicit verification state such as VERIFIED-BY-USE, BUILT-UNVERIFIED, or BUILT.
Projections are read-only session lenses; structural ones are GPU-free, while semantic ones depend on an external :8007 embedding index.
Control verbs implement operator-in-the-loop interference: gate, resume, abort, rewind, and materialize_at_point.
Durable records are stored as JSON or Markdown files under .data/clones, .data/gates, and .data/channels.
The file frames its own role as finding and reporting existing code, not drawing conclusions or making build plans.
standouts: The headline that the rich, addressable substrate is mostly built but has no rendered Face consuming it, described as built wire, unrendered face.
The verification-state discipline that explicitly separates code-presence from proven-by-use.
The recurring Tim-pattern reference: built capability that remains unsurfaced in the UI.
conventions: Uses a FINDS header with trust, author, host quotes, and date.
Organizes content into labeled sections: NODES, EDGES, PROJECTIONS, CONTROL VERBS, LIVE ONBOARDING, DURABLE RECORDS, and THE GAP.
Cites file paths and line numbers for each capability.
Uses uppercase labels for verification states and section headers.
concerns: Several key capabilities are marked BUILT-UNVERIFIED, including mind registry discovery, semantic projections, and onboarding/fleet functions.
The file repeatedly notes that projections have not been proven by execution.
The gap assertion is itself a finding and has not been validated by a UI audit.
notes: A fork-segment inventory of the graph-of-minds substrate, emphasizing what is built, what is verified, and what remains unrendered.
questions: What exactly is the graph-of-minds Face and where is its specification?
Which component owns creating the missing rendered Face?
What is the :8007 embedding index and how is it provisioned?
What does Tim-pattern refer to beyond built but unsurfaced?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to report findings about the existing substrate without drawing conclusions; the file matches that purpose by cataloging nodes, edges, projections, verbs, and records with verification states and identifying the missing Face.
apparent_themes: Substrate-first design: build addressable graph wiring before surfacing a UI.
Verification honesty: separate code-presence from proven-by-use.
Navigable company model: sessions, clones, minds, runs, and boards as first-class graph nodes.
Read-only projection lenses for session introspection.
intention_signals: The file intends to make the hidden graph-of-minds substrate visible and legible to the lead, so that a decision about whether to surface it as a Face or fold it into projections can be grounded in what already exists.
novelty: The explicit verification-state taxonomy applied to a pre-built but unrendered Face substrate, plus the built wire, unrendered face framing.
contribution: A structured inventory of existing address grammars, graph edges, session lenses, control verbs, and durable records, annotated with verification states, which a fused system can use to decide how to surface or extend the graph-of-minds capability without rediscovering the code.
summary_for_embedding: This file inventories an already-built graph-of-minds substrate in a fork segment, documenting addressable node types, graph edges, read-only session projections, control verbs, onboarding and channel wiring, durable records, and the absence of a rendered Face consuming it.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to report existing substrate findings with verification states so a lead can decide whether to build a graph-of-minds Face or fold the substrate into projections.
